statute: Importation of Intoxicating Liquors Act, RSC 1985, c I-3

interprovincial_import_default.description: All interprovincial imports of intoxicating liquor must be made by the provincial liquor authority or a designated private corporation

wine_personal_use_exception.description: Individuals may import wine interprovincially for personal use, subject to receiving province's quantity limits

wine_personal_use_scope.description: Personal use wine exception applies only to wine; beer and spirits remain restricted to provincial authority import

transit_sealed_container.description: Liquor transported through a province to another destination must remain in sealed, unopened containers for the entire transit

provincial_authority_supremacy.description: Provinces retain full jurisdiction to regulate possession, movement, and sale of liquor within their borders regardless of federal exceptions

international_import_authority.description: International importation of liquor into Canada must be made by or under the authority of the provincial liquor board

licensed_distiller_blending_exception.description: Licensed distillers and brewers may import interprovincially solely for blending or flavouring, in accordance with the Excise Act 2001 and provincial law

sacramental_medicinal_exception.description: Importation for sacramental or medicinal purposes is exempted from the general prohibition

contravention_penalty_first_offence.description: First offence penalty: fine not exceeding $200, default imprisonment up to 3 months

marketplace_implication.description: A marketplace facilitating alcohol sales across provincial boundaries must ensure all transactions comply with both IILA and the receiving province's liquor authority requirements
marketplace_implication.l2laaf_note: For L2LAAF: all alcohol transactions must be intra-provincial unless the receiving province's rules explicitly permit the import category
